title: Value Investing with Options: Selling Puts to Buy Wonderful Businesses at Better Prices | S08 E18

segments.text: Group health insurance can put businesses in a tough position,
with rising costs and plans that don't fit everyone's needs.
Now, a new form of employer coverage called an ICHRA or ICRA can help.
ICRAs make costs predictable with stable pre-tax contributions,
and they make health plans personal,
because each employee can pick any plan and carrier that meets their needs.
Get coverage you control.
Learn more at ambetterhealth.com slash ICRA.

I'm doing well.

Really well.

When we have, when we're talking value options,
just in case folks don't know, do you want to give us a little explanation?
Yeah.
So basically, we're value investors, so naturally.
So we're looking to buy businesses that are trading at large discounts to intrinsic value
based on extensive fundamental analysis.
And then when we're talking about value options,
it's using simple strategies such as covered calls or cash secured puts to generate income
and reduce risk.
And we're limiting ourselves to stocks that are already undervalued.
And so that's kind of the critical element of this is that it's value investing.
That's the strategy.
The way that we utilize options are just tactics.
For instance, if you like a stock and you, you would be willing to buy it here.
But maybe you're kind of concerned about the overall market or you just like to make it a little safer even.
You might sell a put, a cash secured put on that stock.
And that creates a situation where there's two outcomes, heads or tails.
Heads, if the stocks above that strike price where you sold the put at expiration,
you make your return.
We target over 10% annualized almost always.
Tails, you end up buying the stock if the stocks below your strike price where you sold the put at.
You have owning it and now you have all the upside, the dividends, you could sell covered calls on it.
However, you want to deal with it from there.
But that's kind of the basis of value options.
There are two things that I like about it.
One is that you can choose your entry and exit price for stocks.
You're not necessarily guaranteed to get that entry or exit price.
But that's the other side of the equation that you can make sure you're getting a sufficient return on.
Because when they're cash secured, so if you take, if you want to go long a stock,
you sell a put which I know is a little bit like inside out the way that people think about it.
You buy a put for protection for downside protection.
So you sell a put so you're underwriting the stock if it falls below that particular strike.
But the nice thing is, and there's an example of Buffett doing it with Coca-Cola in the early 1990s.
After it has established its position, it's trading at $39.
He buys a strike at $35 and he gets a dollar 50 per contract.
So if he gets the stock put to him, it's $33.50 against the $39 stock price.
And in that instance, I guess he thought that $33.50 was a good price to take the stock.
Because the premium that he got was only enough to give him about a 6% annualized return, which is pretty thin for the cash that he had to put up.
So he may not have done it cash secured but you would recommend doing it cash secured.
So you've got to put the money into your account.
And the premium gives you a little discount and basically that's your return for the period of time you have it there.
Correct, correct, exactly.
So it's just, it's a attractive way to layer into a stock.
Everyone wants to buy when stocks, they want to buy stocks at cheaper prices, right?
And it's hard to do that mechanically.
Sometimes people will dollar cost average.
Just, you know, they'll have like certain limit prices that they're willing to buy the stock at.
But when you're selling puts, you're kind of manufacturing that in a way where if that stock doesn't drop to your level, you're still able to profit.
Because sometimes you'll, you'll be buying, let's say Google or meta or something like that.
And the stock runs away from you where you can't buy that full position that maybe you wanted, maybe it had already run up, you get in, you, you establish a foothold in the stock.
But you, you haven't built the full one.
You're just a little cautious on it and you're kind of expecting it to drop at some point.
But you might not get that chance.
And so when you sell puts, you're able to create a heads, you win, tails, you win situation as long as you're willing to own the stock.
That's the key difference.
And so necessarily heads, you win, tells you win.
You've got to make sure that you win on both sides before you put the trade on right.
And in the sense that you want to buy the stock cheaper, you know what I mean?
So it's a stock you want to own.
So you're able to manufacture that cheaper entry price into it.
What 100% at all.
And you get an opportunity comes down to the, is the stock that you're buying good?
You, you still have similar lost dynamics.
I mean, if a stock goes to zero on you, of course.
And it's true on the other side too.
If you own the stock and you want to sell.
It's just the reverse process.
You're not necessarily getting hit, but you generate income in the event that you don't trade all the way up.
Right.
What's, why do you think that these securities get mispriced in the, in the option market?
And is it more likely to mispriced than the underlying?
Oh, I think that yeah, times, that is the case.
And I think it's because just like there's the efficient market theory where, you know, volatility.
Equated to risk in some academic circles.
And I know that someone like Buffett or Charlie Munger would totally agree with that.
Disagree with that.
And so would I personally?
Same thing with options.
I mean, options are priced off a bunch of mathematical algorithms, including volatility as being a big component of that.
So if a stock drops a lot, the price of that option might be very, very high.
But that doesn't necessarily mean that the underlying stock is extremely risky or that the option position that you're building is risky.
I mean, we see things where you might have like a real estate investment trust that's trading at 50% of its net asset value.
And it's got a good balance sheet.
Obviously, there's headwinds, right?
Because why is it trading so cheaply?
Even now, I mean, even high quality ones are trading at 15, 20, 25% discounts to NAV just because real estate is so out of favor with rates going up.
And then you're, you know, they've taken a big hit.
So volatility is pretty high.
You're able to sell puts way, way out of the money.
So creating a much, much, much larger margin of safety to what you already see as a pretty big margin of safety.
So you're kind of benefiting two ways.
You have the misprice stock.
And then you have the option that's just really based on the mathematical principles of how far the stock dropped in what amount of time and the perceptions of short term movements with options.
And I think it gives the advantage to someone that's patient that's willing to own the underlying.
And that's one of the things that that we do at value options letter is that we break down the mathematics for subscribers so that they can see, okay, we're targeting 13% on this trade.
In the worst case, you're going to manufacture an entry price 25% below the current price.
This is why this stock looks attractive.
These are the fundamentals behind it.
And then those subscribers also get input on when good exiting opportunities would be.
So it's easier to track the options.

Well, I mean, how do you measure the risk when you buy stock?
Right?
So I mean, the number one risk we're concerned with is mitigating

So that's the big difference in how we use options versus many people

Or they'll do things like technical trading and they're doing spreads

So let's start a little bit with what railroads used to look like.
And for most of their history, North American railroads were less like factories
and more like a jazz band.
Local managers would make these judgment calls, trains would leave
when they were just full enough basically in the opinion of that person.
Railyards acted like these giant buffers,
where they would soak up a lot of uncertainty and delay.
And it was organized in a very improvisational way.
And for a long time, that worked because competitive pressures just weren't really that intense.
But then trucking got better deregulation finally arrived.
And investors started demanding higher returns.
And then we come to this guy Hunter Harrison.
And he's one of the most divisive operators in transportation history.
He ran four class one railroads in his career.
And unfortunately, he died three weeks after taking over CSX
But the basic story with him was he would come in and take over.
And all of a sudden the operating ratios, which is basically just like profit margin,
the term that they use in railroads,
would just continue to keep improving.
And he would take a company or a railroad that was earning like 2%,
two pennies on every dollar of revenue,
and bring it down to like, you know, 40 cents of profit margin.
Just absolutely huge.
He did like four different times in different locations.
And this philosophy that he implemented was called precision schedule railroading PSR.
And you might have heard Buffett talk about this at different points.
And it's a pretty common, there's a common argument about this.
So what PSR actually does is it takes what the traditional railroads used to do
was wait for the demand to show up and then ship something.
PSR forces the customer to adopt to the schedule of the train is leaving,
get your stuff on there, otherwise sorry.
You know, the train leaves on a clock whether it's full or not.
And they try to run fewer trains, they run longer trains.
And the whole point is that less idle equipment, less dwell time is what it's called.
You know, where a rail car is just sitting motionless in the yard.
And this whole network is then tuned for one continuous thing, which is just low.
And the railroad stops behaving like a freight company.
It starts looking like a circulatory system, which is where we're going to go back to the octopus.
And believe it or not, an octopus has three hearts, two pump blood to the gills,
one pumps oxygenated blood throughout the body.
And more importantly, an octopus is this profoundly decentralized organism.
No rigid skeleton, eight arms that are operating semi independently.
A huge percentage of the neurons live in the arms themselves not in some central brain.
And each arm can then solve problems and react locally on its own.
But there's this famous problem in anything decentralized, which is like, how do you coordinate?
And the octopus solve this by building a circulatory architecture that keeps this flow synchronized across the whole organism.
Multiple hearts are managing pressure and the body functions then as one system,
even though the intelligence is kind of spread out.
And this is kind of what PSR is trying to do for a railroad.
Decentralized assets, but centralized circulation.
And the network is this organism.
And it's, you know, it's the flow is the point.
So, but there's a trade off here.
And the octopus, when it swims really hard, its main systemic heart actually struggles under that load.
And which is why you use octopuses prefer to crawl typically rather than like swim.
It's very expensive, metabolically to swim.
And they weren't really built for it.
So that same trade off sits inside of PSR.
Traditional railroads actually carried a lot of slack.
They had extra crews, extra locomotives, extra yard capacity.
And on paper, that slack looks like waste, of course.
But in practice, it's a bit like biological fat reserves, like it absorbs shocks.
And PSR strips most of that out.
The financial results are amazing.
And the operating ratios drop to be attractive, assets get more productive, free cashflow gushes.
But you also have removed this buffer.
And one delayed train then can pressure into the next.
One congested yard creates, you know, downstream choke points.
And the same synchronization that produces, that it produces efficiency can also then amplify fragility.
So kind of the investing version of this that we've seen, you know, relatively recently was.
In Japanese auto parts in 2011 with the 9.0, you know, magnitude earthquake hitting Japan.
It was a triple disaster because yeah, the quake, the tsunami, and Fukushima meltdown.
And these manufacturing companies in Japan were really like the gold standard of just-in-time delivery.
You know, Toyota basically invented this.
And the average Japanese manufacturer at the time held only three weeks of material inventory.
So really lean balance sheets, lean warehouses, single source suppliers, look like this super efficient industrial system.
And of course, MBAs rejoiced at how well they were taking working capital out of it.
But, but then it stopped.
And production fell 78% year over year in April of 2011 because of this earthquake.
And it wasn't that Toyota's own plants were even destroyed.
It was actually their suppliers, suppliers were destroyed.
And that's then chokes the whole system.
So manufacturing actually dropped across Japan completely 15% of March.
It didn't recover for like six months.
So after that, Toyota rewrote their business continuity plans.
And they now require key suppliers to hold anywhere from two to six months of inventory minimum.
And they moved away from single sourcing many of their things.
And, you know, there's some irony in the fact that the firm invented just in time.
Volunteerly put slack back into the system.
And they're slack they sacrifice efficiency on purpose.

And, you know, you see these lower operating margins are better operating margins, I should say in in PSR.
